Dpr Constructions Notice Of Motion And Motion To Stay
Off Calendar Mar-12-2020 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Steve Chasey, Esq. of VARELA LEE METZ & GUARINO LLP, T:415.623.7000, for defendant/ moving party;
William T. Eliopoulos, Esq. of RUTAN & TUCKER LLP, T:650.320.1501, for plaintiff/ opposing party.

RULING: The tentative ruling was contested and argued. After arguments, the Court adopted its tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT DPR CONSTRUCTION'S MOTION TO STAY is denied. A stay is not warranted based on the doctrine of failure to exhaust administrative remedies. Redwood is currently exhausting its administrative remedies by pursuing its writ action. (See Redwood Electric Group, Inc. v. The Regents of the University of California, No. CPF-19-516850.) The gravamen of the instant action pertains to rights unrelated to the administrative substitution hearing. (See JMS Air Conditioning & Appliance Service, Inc. v. Santa Monica Community College Dist. (2018) 30 Cal.App.5th 945, 958 ["[A] section 4107 substitution hearing does not adjudicate anything beyond those limited rights section 4107 created."].) While there is some overlap between the third cause of action in this case and the writ proceeding, there is no salient prejudice in having both actions proceed simultaneously. The court denies a stay based on the primary jurisdiction doctrine. That doctrine applies where an administrative body should resolve matters under its regulatory scheme before the court proceeds. (See Farmers Ins. Exchange v. Superior Court (1992) 2 Cal.4th 377, 390.) In this case, there is nothing for the administrative agency to adjudicate. The court denies the request to stay this action based on its inherent powers given the slight overlap in the issues and lack of prejudice to defendant. However, the Court will entertain a renewed motion, if Redwood does not obtain an adjudication of its petition for writ of mandate within a reasonable time prior to the existing trial date.

Order signed in open court.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Sep-24-2019 Overruled
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
William Eliopoulos, Esq. 650-320-1501, Carrie Macintosh, Esq. 650-320-1513 of Rutan & Tucker for Plaintiff.
Varela Lee, Esq. 415-623-7000 of Varela Lee Metz & Guarino LLP for Defendant DPR CONSTRUCTION.

RULING - A R G U E D. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT DPR CONSTRUCTION'S DEMURRER TO REDWOOD ELECTRIC GROUEP, INC.'S COMPLAINT.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Defendant DPR Construction's demurrer to Plaintiff Redwood Electric Group, Inc.'s complaint is overruled. A demurrer can be used only to challenge defects that appear on the face of the pleading under attack; or from matters outside the pleading that are judicially noticeable. (Blank v. Kirwan (1985) 39 Cal.3d 311, 318.) The Court declines to judicially notice the findings, conclusions, and decision of the hearing officer at the prior substitution hearing or Redwood's hearing brief in connection with that hearing. DPR has not shown that the factual findings in the prior Public Contract Code section 4107 hearing are properly subject to judicial notice. (See JMS Air Conditioning & Appliance Service, Inc. v. Santa Monica Community College Dist. (2018) 30 Cal.App.5th 945, 959 ["the findings in the Substitution Decision do not 'possess a judicial character' and thus have no binding effect in non-section 4107 civil or administrative actions."]; id. at fn. 5 ["because we affirm the Substitution Decision, [the subcontractor] is precluded from suing for violation of section 4107. But this preclusion does not apply to any other actions or defenses."].) Further, "a section 4107 substitution hearing does not adjudicate anything beyond those limited rights section 4107 created. [] Thus, a subcontractor . . . remains free to pursue claims against the prime contractor that are not based on section 4107 statutory rights or duties. For example, if [the subcontractor] believes it was wrongfully terminated under the terms of the subcontract with [the general contractor], it may seek redress under the terms of that contract." (Id. at 958; see also Knickerbocker v. City of Stockton (1988) 199 Cal.App.3d 235, 245-246 [trial court erred in sustaining demurrer without leave to amend where adverse determination in civil service hearing was not fatal to all of plaintiff's causes of action].) As to the second cause of action, which seeks to state a claim for violation of section 4107, the Court takes judicial notice that Plaintiff has filed a petition for administrative mandate of mandate challenging the hearing officer's decision. "A hearing officer's decision is not final and binding where it is reviewable by a trial court under Code of Civil Procedure section 1094.5." (American Federation of State, County & Municipal Employees v. Metropolitan Water Dist. (2005) 126 Cal.App.4th 247, 259; see generally Lucido v. Superior Court (1990) 51 Cal.3d 335, 340 [essential element of collateral estoppel is that "the decision in the former proceeding must be final and on the merits"].) Plaintiff's complaint sufficiently alleges facts to support the first, second, third, and fifth causes of action.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)
